generator: NOS Vulnerability Scanner v1 — Autonomous Misconfiguration Analysis

findings.title: Docker socket mounted in Portainer — full host control
findings.detail: Portainer mounts /var/run/docker.sock without read-only flag. Any container escape or Portainer RCE gives full Docker API access = root on host. Combined with network-facing web UI, this is the #1 attack vector.
findings.remediation: Add :ro flag. Consider Docker socket proxy (tecnativa/docker-socket-proxy) to limit API surface. Restrict Portainer to Tier 1 admins only (already done via RBAC).

findings.title: Docker socket mounted in Woodpecker CI agent
findings.detail: Woodpecker agent mounts Docker socket for pipeline execution. Any malicious pipeline can escape to host. No read-only flag.
findings.remediation: Use rootless Docker backend or container-based isolation. Pin Woodpecker to specific version. Restrict who can push pipelines in Gitea.

findings.title: Traefik mounts Docker socket read-only — still risky
findings.detail: Traefik mounts Docker socket as :ro for service discovery. Read-only mitigates some attacks but Docker API read access still leaks env vars (secrets, passwords) of all containers.
findings.remediation: Use Docker socket proxy (tecnativa/docker-socket-proxy) with only GET requests to /containers allowed. Alternatively switch to file-based provider.

findings.title: Redis has NO authentication — accessible cross-stack
findings.detail: Redis runs without password (requirepass not set). It's on shared_net accessible to all containers. Any compromised container can read/write Redis data (session tokens, cache, queue data for Authentik, ERPNext, Outline, Superset).
findings.remediation: Add --requirepass {{ redis_password }} to Redis command. Update all clients (Authentik, ERPNext, Outline, Superset) to use redis://:<password>@redis:6379. Add redis_password to credentials.yml.

findings.title: Home Assistant can run in privileged mode
findings.detail: When homeassistant_privileged=true, container runs with full host privileges + host network. Gated by config flag (default: false), but if enabled, complete container escape is trivial.
findings.remediation: Document the risk clearly. Add Ansible warning when homeassistant_privileged=true. Consider capabilities-based approach instead (NET_ADMIN, NET_RAW only).

findings.title: No memory/CPU limits on any Docker service
findings.detail: None of the 35+ Docker services have mem_limit, cpus, or deploy.resources constraints. A single misbehaving service (GitLab OOM, ERPNext memory leak, Ollama model loading) can starve all other services and crash the host.
findings.remediation: Add resource limits per service tier: critical services 2GB/2CPU, medium 1GB/1CPU, low 512MB/0.5CPU. Start with GitLab (known memory hog: recommend 4GB limit) and ERPNext (6 containers, recommend 1GB each).

findings.title: All 30+ passwords use predictable changeme_pw_* pattern
findings.detail: Every password in default.credentials.yml follows the pattern changeme_pw_{service}. While overridden by credentials.yml (gitignored) in production, a blank=true reinstall with missing credentials.yml leaves all services with guessable passwords.
findings.remediation: The auto-generate secrets feature in main.yml handles this for blank runs (prompts for prefix). Verify that ALL services are covered by auto-generation. Add ansible-lint rule to fail if changeme_pw_ appears in generated configs.

findings.title: Calibre-Web ships with hardcoded admin/admin123 default
findings.detail: LinuxServer Calibre-Web image uses admin/admin123 default. No automated password change mechanism. If deployed behind Authentik proxy auth, the default password still works on the service directly.
findings.remediation: Add post-provisioning task to change Calibre-Web admin password via API or sqlite3 manipulation. Or document as mandatory manual step.

findings.title: FreePBX SIP/RTP ports bound to all interfaces
findings.detail: SIP (5060/udp+tcp), IAX (4569/udp), and RTP (10000-10100/udp) are bound to 0.0.0.0, exposing VoIP protocols to the entire network. SIP brute-force and toll fraud are common attack vectors.
findings.remediation: Bind SIP/IAX to 127.0.0.1 unless LAN VoIP is required. Use Fail2Ban (already enabled). Consider SIP over TLS (5061) and SRTP. Add firewall rules for RTP range.

findings.title: 23 Docker images use :latest tag — no version reproducibility
findings.detail: 23 of 36 Docker services use :latest or :stable/:main tags. Docker pull can silently introduce breaking changes or supply chain compromises. No image digest pinning.
findings.evidence: Affected: grafana, prometheus, loki, portainer, traefik, bluesky_pds, infisical, wordpress, n8n, gitea, gitlab, jellyfin, calibreweb, rustfs, tileserver, puter, vaultwarden, freescout, freepbx, qgis_server, metabase, superset, paperclip
findings.remediation: Pin all critical+high services to specific semver tags. Use image digest (@sha256:...) for highest assurance. Implement scheduled version check to detect available updates.

findings.title: Unofficial Docker images from tiredofit/* registry
findings.detail: FreeScout and FreePBX use images from tiredofit/* (single maintainer, not official project images). Supply chain risk: if maintainer account is compromised, malicious images could be pushed.
findings.remediation: Monitor tiredofit/* repos for unusual activity. Consider building from source or switching to official images when available. Pin to specific version tags + digest.

findings.title: Open WebUI uses :main branch tag — unstable, unaudited
findings.detail: Open WebUI tracks the :main branch tag, which points to latest development builds. These may contain unreviewed code, regressions, or vulnerabilities not yet caught by CI.
findings.remediation: Switch to a specific release tag (e.g., 0.6.x). The :main tag should only be used for development/testing.

findings.title: ERPNext uses MariaDB root credentials directly
findings.detail: All ERPNext containers (6 total) have DB_ROOT_USER=root and DB_ROOT_PASSWORD in environment. If any ERPNext container is compromised, attacker gets full MariaDB root access to all databases.
findings.remediation: Create dedicated MariaDB user for ERPNext with minimal privileges. ERPNext configurator needs root for initial setup, but runtime containers should use limited user.

findings.title: No TLS/SSL between Docker services and databases
findings.detail: Database connections from all services to MariaDB, PostgreSQL, and Redis use unencrypted TCP over Docker networks. While Docker network isolation provides some protection, a compromised container on shared_net can sniff all database traffic.
findings.evidence: No SSL-related environment variables for database connections. Outline explicitly sets PGSSLMODE: disable
findings.remediation: Enable SSL on PostgreSQL and MariaDB. Distribute mkcert CA cert to client containers. Set PGSSLMODE=require for PostgreSQL clients.

findings.title: host-gateway extra_hosts enables container-to-host access
findings.detail: Services with extra_hosts: host.docker.internal:host-gateway can access all host-side services (Ollama, Nginx, SSH, any localhost-bound port). Used by: Grafana, n8n, Open WebUI, Woodpecker, GitLab, Vaultwarden.
findings.remediation: Acceptable for Ollama access and Authentik OIDC. Document which services need host access and why. Consider running Ollama in Docker to eliminate this vector.

findings.title: Inconsistent log rotation limits across services
findings.detail: Log max-size varies: 10m (Redis, Uptime Kuma, Calibre-Web, Kiwix, RustFS, Tileserver, QGIS, ERPNext workers), 20m (most services), 50m (GitLab). No aggregate limit. A log flood in one service could fill disk.
findings.evidence: GitLab: max-size 50m x 5 files = 250MB. ERPNext: 6 containers x 10m x 3 = 180MB. Total potential: ~2GB+ logs
findings.remediation: Standardize to 20m/5 files for all services. Consider centralizing all logs via Alloy→Loki (already partially done) and reducing local retention.

findings.title: Fixed secret keys in default.credentials.yml
findings.detail: Secret keys (Outline SECRET_KEY/UTILS_SECRET, Superset SECRET_KEY, Open WebUI SECRET_KEY, jsos SESSION_SECRET) use fixed changeme_pw_* strings. If not overridden in credentials.yml, session tokens and encrypted data are predictable.
findings.remediation: main.yml already auto-generates some secrets on blank run. Verify coverage: ensure ALL secret_key/utils_secret/auth_secret variables are auto-generated with openssl rand -hex 32.

findings.title: X-XSS-Protection header is deprecated
findings.detail: X-XSS-Protection: 1; mode=block is deprecated and can introduce vulnerabilities in some browsers. Modern browsers ignore it. Content-Security-Policy is the replacement.
findings.remediation: Remove X-XSS-Protection header. Add Content-Security-Policy header with appropriate directives per vhost (default-src 'self' as baseline).

findings.title: Shared Docker network allows unrestricted inter-service communication
findings.detail: All services connected to stacks_shared_network can communicate freely. A compromised WordPress can reach PostgreSQL, Redis, Authentik, and all other services directly.
findings.evidence: Every stack's compose file connects services to {{ stacks_shared_network }} (external: true)
findings.remediation: Consider micro-segmentation: separate networks for data tier (DB+Redis), identity tier (Authentik), and application tier. Use Docker network aliases for controlled access.

findings.title: Nginx server_tokens off — good, but upstream services may leak versions
findings.detail: Nginx correctly hides its version (server_tokens off). However, upstream services may expose versions via Server headers, X-Powered-By, or API endpoints (e.g., Gitea /api/v1/version).
findings.remediation: Add proxy_hide_header to strip X-Powered-By, Server, X-AspNet-Version from upstream responses. Audit each service for version-leaking endpoints.

findings.title: Redis maxmemory 256MB — may be insufficient for multi-service usage
findings.detail: Redis serves 5+ services (Authentik, ERPNext cache+queue+socketio, Outline, Superset). 256MB with allkeys-lru eviction may cause premature key eviction under load, leading to session loss or cache misses.
findings.remediation: Monitor Redis memory usage via Grafana. Consider increasing to 512MB or 1GB. Alternatively, separate Redis instances per service (ERPNext uses 3 separate DB numbers already).
